PhD Researcher · Surgical Robotics

Jesse
d'Almeida

Vanderbilt University · Vanderbilt MEDLab

Building robots that help surgeons heal. I develop minimally invasive continuum robotic systems for surgical applications — from transendoscopic suturing to fibroid removal — bridging mechanical design, kinematics, and clinical need.

Google Scholar MEDLab LinkedIn
NSF Graduate
Research Fellow

Background

About Me

I'm a PhD student in Mechanical Engineering at Vanderbilt University, where I work in the Medical Engineering and Discovery Lab (MEDLab) under Dr. Robert J. Webster III. My research focuses on continuum and concentric tube robots for minimally invasive surgery — systems that can navigate the body's natural pathways to reach places traditional tools cannot.

I completed my MS and BS in Robotics Engineering at Worcester Polytechnic Institute (WPI), where I worked with Dr. Loris Fichera on accessible concentric tube robot designs and steerable laser fibers for office-based laryngeal surgery.

I'm passionate about the full arc from blue-sky research to clinical impact. My work spans robot design and fabrication, kinematic modeling, surgical workflow integration, and collaboration directly with surgeons on phantom and tissue experiments.

jesse.f.dalmeida@vanderbilt.edu LinkedIn Google Scholar

Selected Work

Featured Publications

2025 journal

Robotic Transurethral Vesicourethral Anastomosis after Radical Prostatectomy in Validated Models

J. F. d'Almeida, K. Maciolek, E. Wilke, J. Shrand, L. Shepard, T. E. Ertop, A. E. Ghazi, R. J. Webster III, S. D. Herrell III, N. L. Kavoussi

Journal of Endourology (JEndo)

Techniques that minimize dissection of neurovascular structures during radical prostatectomy improve perioperative outcomes and functional recovery. We sought to address this with a concentric tube robot (CTR) system designed specifically for performing a transurethral vesicourethral anastomosis after transurethral prostatectomy, in a series of validated phantoms.

Open PDF -> Jump to full entry ->
2026 conference

Needle-and-Thread Suturing With Concentric Tube Robots for Tracheal Stent Fixation

J. F. d'Almeida, L. Branscombe, A. O. Chara, S. Stern, F. Maldonado, A. Kuntz, and R. J. Webster III

ISMR 2026

We present a teleoperated suturing system for tracheal stent fixation using concentric tube robots, demonstrating precise needle control and secure fixation in a validated airway phantom.

Open PDF -> Jump to full entry ->

Watch

Research in Action

Projects

Research Focus

Transendoscopic Robotic Suturing

Developing a dual-arm handheld concentric tube robot system for Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (NOTES), enabling suturing in confined anatomical spaces without external incisions.

Lead Project · 2021–Present
Expand

This project develops a dual-arm handheld concentric tube robot (CTR) system designed for transendoscopic suturing in Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (NOTES). The system is designed to pass through the working channel of a standard clinical endoscope, enabling minimally invasive repair of internal tissues — such as anastomotic leaks or prostatectomy closures — without external incisions. Key contributions include novel end-effector designs optimized for confined working spaces, kinematic modeling under tissue loading, and direct validation with surgeons on realistic surgical phantoms and biological tissue.

ALISS: Autonomy at a Less Invasive Scale

Collaborating on automation methods for suturing and hemostasis in minimally invasive surgery. Co-authored the proposal for an ARPA-H award of up to $12 million.

Vanderbilt MEDLab · 2024–Present
Expand

ALISS (Autonomy at a Less Invasive Scale) is a multi-institutional effort funded by ARPA-H (up to $12M) focused on automating key surgical tasks — specifically suturing and hemostasis — in minimally invasive procedures. The goal is to enhance surgical accuracy and reduce procedure time through intelligent robotic assistance. Contributions include co-authoring the initial ARPA-H proposal, collecting and organizing surgical model datasets for machine learning training, and developing automation pipelines for endoscopic interventions.

Side-Firing Robot for Intrauterine Fibroid Removal

Leading development of a continuum robot for resecting fibroids inside the uterus, modeling concentric tube robot kinematics at the superelastic limit for high-curvature arms.

Lead Researcher · 2024–Present
Expand

Uterine fibroids are among the most common benign tumors in women, often requiring invasive hysterectomy or open myomectomy. This project develops a novel side-firing continuum robot capable of navigating through a hysteroscope and resecting fibroid tissue from within the uterine cavity. A key research challenge is modeling the kinematics of concentric tube robots operating at their superelastic limit — enabling dramatically tighter curvatures than conventional designs and allowing the robot to work perpendicular to the endoscope axis ("sideways").

MRI-Guided Focal Prostate Resection

Contributing to a feasibility study for low-field MRI-guided transurethral robotic focal prostate resection, bridging imaging and robotic intervention for precision oncology.

Collaborative · 2022–Present
Expand

Focal therapy for prostate cancer requires precise targeting of tumor tissue while sparing surrounding healthy anatomy. This project investigates the feasibility of using low-field intraoperative MRI to guide a transurethral concentric tube robot for focal resection. Working with high-field pre-operative images as a proxy, we demonstrated the feasibility of robot workspace coverage of clinically relevant tumor locations.

Open-Source 3D-Printed Concentric Tube Robot

MS thesis creating an accessible concentric tube robot from 3D-printed and off-the-shelf materials, enabling broader educational adoption without expensive custom fabrication.

MS Thesis · WPI 2021
Expand

Concentric tube robots (CTRs) are powerful tools for minimally invasive surgery, but their fabrication traditionally requires specialized superelastic NiTi tubing and precision machining — limiting access for researchers and educators. This thesis investigates whether additive manufacturing (Multi Jet Fusion, FDM) can replicate the kinematic behavior of traditional NiTi CTRs. The result is an open-source CTR design made entirely from 3D-printed components and off-the-shelf materials, capable of actuating three precurved stages, validated against the standard Cosserat rod kinematic model.

Steerable Laser Fibers for Office-Based Laryngology

Developed simulation platforms and end-effector designs to steer a laser fiber through an endoscope into the larynx for treatment of pre-malignant vocal fold tumors. Awarded Best Student Paper at SPIE 2021.

WPI Research · 2019–2021
Expand

Office-based laser surgery of the vocal folds offers a promising alternative to traditional operating room procedures for treating pre-malignant lesions. This work developed a simulation platform for evaluating steerable laser fiber designs within anatomical meshes of the larynx, quantifying reachable workspace and dosimetry coverage. A key finding was that angled fiber tip geometries significantly outperform flat-cleaved tips in targeting accuracy. The simulation framework was validated against an ex vivo laryngoscopic procedure model. This work received the Best Student Paper Award at SPIE Medical Imaging 2021.

On the merits of using angled fiber tips in office-based laser surgery of the vocal folds
I. A. Chan*, J. F. d'Almeida*, M. Zhu, A. J. Chiluisa, Y. Shen, Y. Liu, T. L. Carroll, L. Fichera, C. A. Linte, and J. H. Siewerdsen
SPIE Medical Imaging 2021
★ Best Student Paper · *co-first authors
DOI

We investigate the merits of angled fiber tips for delivering laser energy to vocal fold lesions during office-based laryngoscopic procedures, demonstrating improved targeting accuracy and tissue coverage compared to flat-cleaved fibers.

News & Activities

Highlights

talk
2024
Keynote: Discovering Surgical Robotics — From First Steps to Breakthroughs
Invited keynote speaker at the TransformA Congress for Biomedical Engineering Students at Universidad Anáhuac Mayab in Mérida, Mexico. Presented Vanderbilt MEDLab's work in surgical robotics and shared what research looks like across career stages — inspiring the next generation of biomedical engineers. Presented in both English and Spanish.
Mérida, Mexico · Universidad Anáhuac Mayab
grant
2024
ARPA-H Award: Autonomy at a Less Invasive Scale (ALISS) — up to $12M
Co-authored the initial proposal for a landmark multi-institutional surgical autonomy project led by Dr. Robert Webster III. The project — focused on automating suturing and hemostasis in minimally invasive surgery — was successfully awarded up to $12 million from the Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health (ARPA-H).
Vanderbilt University · MEDLab
award
2022
NSF Graduate Research Fellowship (GRFP)
Recipient of the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship Program, one of the most prestigious graduate fellowships in the US, recognizing outstanding research potential in surgical robotics and medical engineering.
National Science Foundation
award
2021
Best Student Paper — SPIE Medical Imaging 2021
Awarded Best Student Paper (co-first author) for work on angled laser fiber tips in office-based vocal fold surgery. The paper demonstrated that angled fiber geometries significantly improve targeting accuracy for pre-malignant lesion treatment.
SPIE Medical Imaging Conference
paper
2025
Paper Accepted: Needlescopic Robot for Pediatric Esophageal Repair · Hamlyn 2025
Paper accepted at the Hamlyn Symposium on Medical Robotics presenting a needlescopic robot designed to enable minimally invasive closed-chest repair of congenital tracheal-esophageal defects — a first-of-its-kind robotic approach to this pediatric surgical challenge.
Hamlyn Symposium on Medical Robotics · 2025
outreach
Ongoing
Multilingual Science Communication
A native English and advanced Portuguese speaker with intermediate Spanish, Jesse actively engages international audiences — including presenting in Spanish at conferences in Latin America to broaden the reach of surgical robotics research beyond English-speaking institutions.
English · Portuguese · Spanish
